text: This gentleman presented to the emergency department with right leg pain, abdominal pain with peritonitis and bowel obstruction.
He was found to have a herniation of his right iliac artery with small bowel.
His history includes having a prostatectomy with lymphadenectomy with large amount of dissection which likely caused the potential hernia space along the right iliac.
I show several segments during the procedure of multiple maneuvers that I accomplished
Val is stuck underneath the iliac.
The aorta is visually identified in the surgical field.
What's really scary about this case was that during the operation, I had not pre-operatively identified this was an internal herniation of the iliac artery.
And it appeared that this was a simple band on examination during the exploratory laparotomy.
There was no pulse on it.
The surgeon uses a bovie or ligature device to transect across the identified structure, likely addressing the internal herniation of the iliac artery.
However, I felt that the artery was very close by and I just didn't have a good feeling about it.
I decided to do it a little bit more dissection and luckily I did as this could have been catastrophic.
Taking the extra time at three o'clock in the morning and having that patience to do this saved me in this case.
It's very humbling and if you ever encounter a similar situation, be advised that you may not feel a pulsatile band and pre-operatively have to have a high index of suspicion.
Literature review demonstrates there's a handful of cases that have followed in a similar pattern.
